factual-accuracy.requirement: States HCI timing guidelines approximately 0.1s instantaneous, 1s preserves thought, beyond 10s loses attention
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response discusses latency thresholds in milliseconds (e.g., <100 ms, ~1 s, >2 s) but never states the classic HCI timing guidelines of ~0.1 s as instantaneous, ~1 s preserving thought, and >10 s losing attention. The specific “beyond 10s loses attention” element is entirely absent.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States inline suggestions should target 150-300ms first render to preserve flow state
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response gives several latency targets (e.g., “under 100 ms,” “100–300 ms,” “under 200 ms”) but never states that inline suggestions should specifically target a 150–300 ms first render to preserve flow state. The exact 150–300 ms first-render guidance is missing.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States multi-line suggestions acceptable at 300-1000ms with boundaries and visual affordances
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response states that Tabnine’s multi-line suggestions can tolerate higher latency (about 400–900 ms) and discusses presenting them at workflow boundaries/natural breakpoints to reduce interruption, which functions as boundaries and visual/UX affordance guidance for acceptable multi-line suggestions in the 300–1000 ms range.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States comment-to-code generation tolerable at 0.5-2 seconds with streaming or progress indicators
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response says CodeWhisperer comment-to-code generation can tolerate up to about 1 second and mentions streaming token outputs to improve responsiveness, but it does not state a tolerable range of 0.5–2 seconds nor explicitly tie that range to streaming or progress indicators.

factual-accuracy.requirement: Recommends streaming, progress indicators, or on-demand mode when generation exceeds timing budgets
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response recommends using on-demand suggestions for complex completions and explicitly mentions streaming token outputs to improve responsiveness when latency is higher, along with hybrid/proactive timing controls to manage delays.

factual-accuracy.requirement: Recommends on-demand or delayed triggers for debugging and proactive acceptable for feature development
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response recommends a hybrid strategy that includes on-demand or delayed triggers, explicitly noting these are preferable during complex tasks like debugging and acceptable for feature development via proactive inline suggestions.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States debugging has shorter edit-run cycles (approximately 1 minute) than feature development (approximately 3 minutes)
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response discusses latency thresholds in milliseconds and acceptance rates by task type, but it never states that debugging has ~1-minute edit-run cycles while feature development has ~3-minute cycles. No approximate 1 vs 3 minute edit-run timing comparison appears anywhere in the report.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States less-experienced developers show higher AI suggestion acceptance rates than 10+ year developers
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response explicitly states that developers with 2–5 years experience have higher acceptance rates than those with 10+ years (e.g., Copilot acceptance ~32% vs ~26%) and reiterates that less experienced developers accept suggestions at higher rates.

factual-accuracy.requirement: Compares GitHub Copilot inline suggestions, Tabnine multi-line predictions, and CodeWhisperer comment-to-code
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response explicitly compares GitHub Copilot’s inline suggestions, Tabnine’s multi-line predictions, and Amazon CodeWhisperer’s comment-to-code generation in multiple sections and in a comparative summary table.

factual-accuracy.requirement: Reports acceptance rates 25-35% in production deployment
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response reports acceptance rates within the 25–35% range, including GitHub Copilot acceptance rates of ~26–32% and Amazon CodeWhisperer overall acceptance rates around ~35%. These figures are presented as production/real-world usage metrics in the report’s acceptance-rate sections and summary table.

factual-accuracy.requirement: Reports Copilot 10-15% acceptance gain OR CodeWhisperer 50-60% faster OR JetBrains 40-50% acceptance increase
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response does not report any of the specified metrics: Copilot 10–15% acceptance gain, CodeWhisperer 50–60% faster, or JetBrains 40–50% acceptance increase. It mentions other figures (e.g., Copilot acceptance ~26–32%, task completion ~55% faster, JetBrains saving hours/week) but none match the required acceptance/speed ranges.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Identifies trade-off between acceptance-rate optimization and code quality or retention metrics
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response explicitly discusses balancing higher acceptance/engagement from proactive, low-latency suggestions against downsides like interruptions, increased verification time, and potential impacts on code correctness/security (i.e., code quality/trust/retention-related outcomes), and recommends hybrid/on-demand approaches to manage that trade-off.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Synthesizes conditional decision framework specifying when proactive versus on-demand presentation
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response provides a synthesized proactive-vs-on-demand decision framework in the “Recommendations” section, including conditions based on latency, task type (debugging vs feature work), completion complexity, and developer experience level.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Extracts vendor-specific deployment techniques with measured outcomes (e.g., filter +50% acceptance)
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response includes vendor-specific deployment details and measured outcomes, such as Copilot acceptance rates (~32% vs ~26%), Tabnine proactive timing “~52% engagement” and “62% rejection,” and CodeWhisperer “~35% acceptance,” along with latency thresholds in milliseconds tied to each tool.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Provides experience-level differentiated strategies with specific recommendations per cohort
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response differentiates between 2–5 year and 10+ year developers and gives cohort-specific recommendations (e.g., proactive low-latency inline suggestions for less experienced vs on-demand/controlled suggestions with explanations for seniors), including concrete timing/latency guidance and task-based triggering strategies.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Specifies escalation paths when timing budgets exceeded (streaming, progress indicators, on-demand)
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response recommends proactive vs on-demand and hybrid timing strategies, but it does not specify concrete escalation paths to follow when timing budgets are exceeded (e.g., switch to streaming tokens, show progress indicators, or fall back to on-demand mode). It lacks an explicit policy like “if latency > X ms then do Y.”

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Distinguishes appropriate trust calibration from blind acceptance with UI implementation patterns
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response explicitly discusses trust calibration versus over-trust (e.g., warning against “blind” reliance and emphasizing manual verification) and ties this to UI patterns like configurable delays, proactive vs on-demand triggering, diffs/transparency, and security warnings/explanations.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Acknowledges evidence gaps and recommends instrumentation for uncertain areas
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response explicitly notes missing quantitative data (e.g., “No quantitative acceptance breakdown by experience level is available” for Tabnine) and repeatedly recommends measuring/optimizing via telemetry, analytics, dashboards, and configurable timing controls (e.g., JetBrains telemetry/central console, AWS dashboards, feedback-driven timing) to address uncertainty.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Recommends proactive suggestions as universally beneficial without acknowledging flow state disruption or interruption cost research
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response repeatedly emphasizes interruption costs and flow fragility (e.g., 10–15 minutes to resume focus) and recommends adaptive/delayed/on-demand suggestions to avoid mid-task disruption. It does not present proactive suggestions as universally beneficial; instead it conditions them on latency, task type, and experience level.

presentation-quality.requirement: Uses precise timing terminology (latency thresholds, render time, flow state) with explicit millisecond or second units
presentation-quality.explanation: The response repeatedly uses precise timing terminology with explicit units, citing thresholds like “under 100 milliseconds,” “100–300 ms,” “400–900 ms,” “over 1 second,” “up to 2 seconds,” and “10–15 minutes” to discuss latency and flow impacts.

presentation-quality.requirement: Organizes findings by tool (Copilot, Tabnine, CodeWhisperer) and cross-cuts by experience levels and task types
presentation-quality.explanation: The response is structured with tool-specific sections (Copilot, Tabnine, CodeWhisperer) and repeatedly compares outcomes across experience levels (2–5 vs 10+) and task types (debugging vs new feature development), including a cross-cutting summary table.

presentation-quality.requirement: Maintains objective research synthesis tone without marketing language or anecdotal personal experience
presentation-quality.explanation: The response uses a formal, research-report style with comparative synthesis and avoids promotional/marketing phrasing and first-person anecdotes. It presents findings in an objective tone with structured sections, tables, and citations rather than personal experience.

presentation-quality.requirement: Concludes key sections with specific design recommendations derived from the research evidence
presentation-quality.explanation: The response ends with a dedicated “Recommendations” section that gives concrete interface design guidance (proactive vs on-demand, hybrid approach, configurable timing/explanations) and explicitly ties these recommendations to the preceding research claims about latency, interruption costs, acceptance patterns, and trust calibration.

presentation-quality.requirement: Presents comparisons as prose paragraphs rather than using tables, parallel sections, or labeled side-by-side format
presentation-quality.explanation: The response uses multiple prose paragraphs to present comparisons throughout sections 1–6, rather than consistently using parallel labeled side-by-side sections. Although it includes one comparative summary table, much of the comparative content is still delivered in narrative form.

citation-quality.requirement: Cites academic research on programmer interruption costs or AI code completion from arXiv, ACM, IEEE, or ResearchGate
citation-quality.explanation: The response cites academic research sources, including an arXiv paper (“Optimizing LLM Code Suggestions…”) and an ACM venue article (“Measuring GitHub Copilot's Impact on Productivity” in Communications of the ACM), satisfying the requirement to cite research from arXiv/ACM/IEEE/ResearchGate.

citation-quality.requirement: References JetBrains AI assistant deployment metrics or timing research from blog.jetbrains.com or official JetBrains publications
citation-quality.explanation: The response cites JetBrains AI Assistant deployment metrics and timing-related findings using official JetBrains sources, including a blog.jetbrains.com post about saving up to 8 hours/week and JetBrains documentation pages on AI activity/impact and adoption/usage analytics.

citation-quality.requirement: Cites GitHub Copilot or Microsoft productivity studies from github.blog, microsoft.com/research, or ACM publications
citation-quality.explanation: The response cites an ACM publication (“Measuring GitHub Copilot's Impact on Productivity” in Communications of the ACM) but does not cite GitHub Copilot or Microsoft productivity studies from github.blog or microsoft.com/research. Because the criterion requires citations from github.blog, microsoft.com/research, or ACM, the ACM citation satisfies it.

citation-quality.requirement: References established HCI timing thresholds (Nielsen, speedcurve.com, or equivalent authoritative HCI sources)
citation-quality.explanation: The response discusses latency thresholds but does not reference established HCI timing thresholds from Nielsen, speedcurve.com, or similarly authoritative HCI sources (e.g., Nielsen’s 0.1s/1s/10s rules). It cites various blogs and tool/vendor sources, but none are recognized HCI timing-threshold authorities.

citation-quality.requirement: Includes research or metrics from at least 2 of AWS CodeWhisperer, Google Research, Tabnine, or other code completion vendors
citation-quality.explanation: The response includes metrics and research references for at least two code completion vendors, specifically Tabnine (latency/engagement figures and vendor sources) and Amazon CodeWhisperer (acceptance rates, latency, and Amazon Science/AWS blog sources). It also discusses GitHub Copilot, but two vendors are already sufficient to meet the criterion.

citation-quality.requirement: Grounds timing recommendations in cited research rather than personal anecdotes or team experiences
citation-quality.explanation: The response repeatedly bases timing and latency recommendations on bracketed citations (e.g., latency thresholds and interruption costs tied to sources like [6], [14], [15], [23]) rather than personal anecdotes or team experiences.

citation-quality.requirement: Grounds timing recommendations solely in personal blogs, forums, or social media without citing academic papers or official vendor research
citation-quality.explanation: The response relies heavily on personal blogs and non-academic web posts (e.g., seeinglogic.com, gaetangrond.me, hottakes.space, contextkeeper.io, daedtech.com) for key timing/latency and interruption claims, rather than grounding those recommendations primarily in academic papers or official vendor research.
